reasoning: The Chicago Fed's National Financial Conditions Credit Subindex is a heavily smoothed macroeconomic time series. Over the past 9 weeks, it has undergone a very distinct and mathematically predictable parabolic decline from a peak of 0.022 on April 11/18 down to 0.002 on May 9. The second derivative has been consistently negative, with recent weekly changes of -0.004, -0.007, and -0.009. Given this strong momentum, it is near-certain that the subindex will be lower than the ~0.002 threshold in the short term (May 18 and June 10), warranting the minimum allowable probability of 0.05. Over the medium term (August), the index is expected to reach the bottom of its 30-to-40-week historical cycle (typically falling far into negative territory, around -0.06 to -0.08); however, left-tail macro risks could trigger a fast credit tightening, increasing uncertainty slightly. Looking out to November 2025, the cycle may have recovered to previous peak ranges, and there is more time for a broader macroeconomic tightening or credit event to manifest. Therefore, the probability increases significantly towards the base rate of ~0.35.
